notes.anchor: Will I, nill I, the ineffable thing has tied me to him
notes.note: Nill is an archaic verb meaning to be unwilling, the negative of will; Webster records it as obsolete except in the old phrase will he, nill he, the ancestor of willy-nilly. Starbuck's wording makes his helplessness total: whether his will consents or refuses, the outcome is the same.

notes.anchor: In that ring Cain struck Abel
notes.note: Genesis 4 records the first murder: Cain kills his brother Abel out of jealousy over God's favor, the act that opens human history with bloodshed. The old Manx sailor lays that primal crime over the crew's brawling ring, hinting that every circle of violence aboard merely repeats the same first sin.

notes.anchor: chasing with curses a Job’s whale round the world
notes.note: Job 41 describes Leviathan, the great sea-beast no man can master: canst thou draw out leviathan with an hook? The book insists no mortal power prevails against it. Calling Moby Dick a Job's whale frames Ahab's hunt as a challenge to the divine order, the very pride the book of Job exists to rebuke.

In Greek myth the nymph Arethusa flees the river-god Alpheus by diving underground and rising again as a freshwater spring at Syracuse in Sicily, a tale of impossible underground passage told in Bulfinch. Ishmael cites it as classical precedent for the whale's rumored secret travel between far-apart seas.

notes.anchor: so like a Caryatid, he patient sits
notes.note: A caryatid is a support column carved as a draped woman bearing the weight of the roof on her head, as on the Athenian Erechtheion (Webster). Picturing the captive king as a living caryatid under the piled entablatures of ages, Melville shows Ahab's buried self crushed beneath the whole weight of history.

notes.anchor: Great Jove himself being made incarnate in a snow-white bull
notes.note: Jove turned himself into a snow-white bull to carry off the princess Europa, one of the most famous transformations in Greek myth, retold in Bulfinch. The beautiful yet terrifying divine animal embodies the very double sense of whiteness Ishmael is tracking: holiness and horror fused in a single color.

Revelation 6:8 names the fourth horseman: behold a pale horse, and his name that sat on him was Death, and Hell followed with him. Ishmael's king of terrors on his pallid horse echoes the verse, showing that even in scripture the ultimate dread is pale, not red.

notes.anchor: bare mention of Whitsuntide marshal in the fancy
notes.note: Whitsuntide is the week of Whit Sunday, the seventh Sunday after Easter, marking Pentecost; Webster notes the name comes from White Sunday, after the white robes of the newly baptized. Ishmael picks it because even this holy white festival can, to a morbid fancy, summon a spectral dread.

The Season-on-the-Line was the yearly stretch, roughly November to February, when sperm whales gathered on the equatorial Pacific grounds the whalemen called the Line. Starbuck's history of the fishery records this seasonality, which Ahab exploits by timing his whole route to reach the Line at exactly the right moment.

notes.anchor: makes him a Prometheus; a vulture feeds upon that heart forever
notes.note: Prometheus stole fire from the gods for mankind and was chained to a rock where a vulture ate his self-renewing liver forever, a myth told in Bulfinch. Ishmael turns it on Ahab: the obsessive thought he himself bred has become the creature that eternally tears at its maker.

notes.anchor: Was not Saul of Tarsus converted from unbelief by a similar fright
notes.note: Acts 9 tells how Saul of Tarsus, the great persecutor of Christians, was flung down by a blinding light on the road to Damascus and rose converted as the apostle Paul. Ishmael makes the conversion a comic proof that sheer awe, whether at holy light or a charging whale, can break the firmest unbelief.

notes.anchor: this were the Loom of Time, and I myself were a shuttle
notes.note: The three Fates of Greek myth spun, measured, and cut the thread of every life on their loom (Bulfinch). Ishmael lays their work over his sword-mat: the fixed warp is necessity, his own shuttle free will, and Queequeg's chance-striking sword is chance, turning a deck chore into a diagram of fate.

notes.anchor: the loggerhead, a stout sort of post rooted in the keel
notes.note: A loggerhead is a short stout post in a whaleboat's stern around which the harpoon line is turned, so its friction can check a running whale without snapping the rope (Webster). Standing about two feet high, it makes an awkward lookout, which is why Flask's perch on it marks his comic overreaching.

notes.anchor: the days that Lazarus lived after his resurrection
notes.note: John 11 tells of Lazarus of Bethany, raised by Jesus four days after his burial. Ishmael treats his own near-drowning as just such a death and return, so the days left to him are clean profit, free of the fear of dying. The allusion makes his calm the cheer of a man who counts himself already dead.

notes.anchor: the angels indeed consorted with the daughters of men
notes.note: Genesis 6:2 says the sons of God saw the daughters of men that they were fair and took them wives; Melville adds that uncanonical Rabbins stretched the verse to devils too, drawing on old Jewish legend of the pre-Flood world. The note marks Fedallah as a being from that border where the human and the uncanny once mixed.
